5: hyperanō de autēs Cheroubein doxēs kataskiazonta to hilastērion — "above it were the cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy seat" (Cheroubein doxēs: cherubim of glory — the genitive doxēs is a genitive of quality: cherubim characterized by glory, glory-cherubim; kataskiazonta: overshadowing, spreading wings over — the cherubim whose wings spread over the mercy seat [Exodus 25:20] are the guardians of the divine presence): hilastērion: the mercy seat (kappōret) where the high priest sprinkled blood on the Day of Atonement — the place of atonement/propitiation. The same word in Romans 3:25 describes Christ as the hilastērion.
7: eis de tēn deuteran hapax tou eniautou monos ho archiereus, ou chōris haimatos, ho prospherei hyper heautou kai tōn tou laou agnoēmatōn — "but into the second only the high priest goes, and he but once a year, and not without taking blood, which he offers for himself and for the unintentional sins of the people" (hapax tou eniautou: once of the year, once per year — the hapax (once) anticipates the contrast with Christ's single sacrifice: the Levitical once-per-year vs. the Christological once-for-all; agnoēmatōn: unintentional sins, inadvertent transgressions — from agnoeo, to be ignorant of; the Levitical atonement covered inadvertent sins specifically, raising the question of intentional sins).
11: Christos de paragenomenos archiereus tōn mellontōn agathōn dia tēs meizōnos kai teleiotera skēnēs ou cheiropioētou, tout' estin ou tautēs tēs ktiseōs — "but when Christ appeared as a high priest of the good things that have come, then through the greater and more perfect tent not made with hands, that is, not of this creation, he entered once for all into the holy places" (tōn mellontōn agathōn: of the good things that have come/are coming — the manuscript tradition is divided between mellontōn [coming/future] and genomenōn [having come/past]; both are theologically defensible): ou cheiropioētou (not made by hands — a technical term for that which belongs to a different ontological order, not constructed by human craft): ou tautēs tēs ktiseōs (not of this creation) — the parenthetical identification of the greater tent as belonging to a different created order from the earthly sanctuary.
12: oude di' haimatos tragōn kai moschōn dia de tou idiou haimatos eisēlthen ephapax eis ta hagia aiōnian lytrōsin heuramenos — "he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption" (ephapax: once for all — the compound of epi + hapax, emphasizing the finality and non-repeatability; aiōnian lytrōsin heuramenos: having found/secured eternal redemption — lytrōsis: the act of ransoming/redeeming, from lytron = ransom price): the contrast is between the animal blood that enables the annual temple ritual and the self-blood that enables the once-for-all heavenly entry. The perfect result (heuramenos = having found, secured) is permanent.
15: kai dia touto diathēkēs kainēs mesitēs estin, hopōs thanatou genomenou eis apolutrōsin tōn epi tē prōtē diathēkē parabaseōn, tēn epangelian labōsin hoi keklēmenoi tēs aiōniou klēronomias — "therefore he is the mediator of a new covenant, so that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance, since a death has occurred that redeems them from the transgressions committed under the first covenant" (apolutrōsin tōn... parabaseōn: redemption-from transgressions — the apo-compound: ransom that moves someone away from the enslaving situation; parabaseōn: transgressions, willful crossings of the boundary, deliberate violations — in contrast to the agnoēmatōn of v7): the death redeems backward (from transgressions under the first covenant) as well as forward (securing the eternal inheritance).
22: kai schedon en haimati panta katharizatai kata ton nomon kai chōris haimatekcysias ou ginetai aphesis — "indeed, under the law almost everything is purified with blood, and without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins" (schedon: almost, nearly — the qualification acknowledges that a few Levitical purifications used water or fire rather than blood [Numbers 31:23; Leviticus 15:10-11]; the general principle holds): chōris haimatekcysias ou ginetai aphesis (without blood-shedding, forgiveness does not occur): haimatekcysia (blood-shedding, bloodshed): a rare compound found only here in the NT; the rule is absolute within the framework of the argument — atonement requires life given (Leviticus 17:11).
24: ou gar eis cheiropioēta eisēlthen hagia Christos, antitypa tōn alēthinōn, all' eis auton ton ouranon, nyn emphanisthēnai tō prosōpō tou theou hyper hēmōn — "for Christ has entered, not into holy places made with hands, which are copies of the true things, but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf" (antitypa tōn alēthinōn: antitypes/copies of the true things — antitypon: the corresponding impression, the copy-form that corresponds to its type): the terminology is inverted from usual typological language: normally the OT type corresponds to the NT antitype; here the earthly sanctuary is the antitypon (the copy, the corresponding-form) of the heavenly original. Nyn emphanisthēnai (now to appear — the present emphasis): Christ is currently appearing in God's presence for the community's benefit.
28: houtōs kai ho Christos hapax prosenechtheis eis to pollōn anenenkein hamartias, ek deuterou chōris hamartias ophthēsetai tois auton apekdechomenois eis sōtērian — "so Christ, having been offered once to bear the sins of many, will appear a second time, not to deal with sin but to save those who are eagerly waiting for him" (hapax prosenechtheis: having been offered once — the once-for-all offering is complete; eis to pollōn anenenkein hamartias: to bear the sins of the many — anaphero: to carry up, to bear [as a sacrifice]: Isaiah 53:12 LXX uses anaphero for the Servant bearing the sins of many): apekdechomenois (eagerly waiting — the same word as Romans 8:19, 23; the eschatological expectation-posture of the community awaiting the parousia).
1: eiche men oun hē prōtē dikaiōmata latreias to te hagion kosmikon — "now the first covenant had regulations for worship and also an earthly sanctuary" (dikaiōmata latreias: regulations/ordinances of worship — dikaiōmata: righteous requirements, legally binding ordinances; the word used in Romans 8:4 for what the Spirit fulfills): to hagion kosmikon (the earthly/worldly sanctuary — kosmikon: belonging to the kosmos, the material world-order): the modifier kosmikon immediately flags the earthly sanctuary as belonging to the temporal, material sphere — as opposed to the heavenly sanctuary of Hebrews 8. The contrast is built into the description from the first word.
2: skēnē gar kateskeuasthē hē prōtē en hē hē te lychnia kai hē trapeza kai hē prothesis tōn artōn, hētis legetai Hagia — "a tabernacle was set up. In its first room were the lampstand and the table with the consecrated bread; this was called the Holy Place" (lychnia: the lampstand — the golden menorah of Exodus 25:31-40; trapeza kai hē prothesis tōn artōn: the table and the setting-out of the loaves — the table of showbread, Exodus 25:23-30, prothesis: the setting-forward, the presentation, the public display): Hagia (Holy Place — the first room, accessible to all priests): the inventory is not merely descriptive but sets up the contrast with what is beyond the second curtain. Each piece of furniture pointed beyond itself to the divine presence.
3: meta de to deuteron katapetasma skēnē hē legomenē Hagia Hagiōn — "behind the second curtain was a room called the Most Holy Place" (meta to deuteron katapetasma: after/behind the second curtain — katapetasma: the hanging veil/curtain; the same word used for the temple veil torn at the crucifixion in the Synoptics): Hagia Hagiōn (Most Holy Place — superlative construct, lit. Holy of Holies; the Hebrew qodesh qodashim): the spatial structure of the tabernacle embodies a theology of graduated holiness — the outer court, the Holy Place, the Most Holy Place — with increasingly restricted access that Hebrews reads as a pointer to the restricted access of the old covenant.
4: chrysoun echousa thymiasētrion kai tēn kibōton tēs diathēkēs periekekkalymmēnēn pantothen chrysiō, en hē stamnos chrysē echousa to manna kai hē rhabdos Aarōn hē blastēsasa kai ai plakes tēs diathēkēs — "this room had the golden altar of incense and the gold-covered ark of the covenant. Inside the ark were the golden jar of manna, Aaron's staff that had budded, and the stone tablets of the covenant" (thymiasētrion: the incense altar or incense-vessel — there is scholarly discussion on whether this refers to the altar of incense or the censer; Hebrews may be associating the incense-altar with the Most Holy Place for theological purposes since the incense was burned in connection with the high priest's entry): rhabdos Aarōn hē blastēsasa (the staff of Aaron that budded — Num 17): the three contents of the ark (manna, Aaron's staff, the covenant tablets) are theologically significant — providential sustenance, divine vindication of priesthood, and the covenant law. Each points to what the new covenant fulfills.
6: toutōn de houtōs kateskeuasmenōn, eis men tēn prōtēn skēnēn dia pantos eisiasin hoi hiereis tas latreias epitelountes — "when everything had been arranged like this, the priests entered regularly into the outer room to carry on their ministry" (dia pantos: continually, at all times — the constant repetition of priestly ministry in the outer room): tas latreias epitelountes (accomplishing/performing the priestly services — epitelountes: present participle, ongoing completion): the daily priestly ministry in the outer room (lampstand tending, incense, showbread) was regular and repeated. The contrast with the inner room (once per year) structures the argument about access and proximity to the divine presence.
8: touto dēlountos tou pneumatos tou hagiou, mēpō pephanerōsthai tēn tōn hagiōn hodon, eti tēs prōtēs skēnēs echousēs stasin — "the Holy Spirit was showing by this that the way into the Most Holy Place had not yet been disclosed as long as the first tabernacle was still standing" (touto dēlountos tou pneumatos tou hagiou: the Holy Spirit making this clear — dēloō: to make manifest, to reveal; the Spirit is the author of the tabernacle's theological meaning): mēpō pephanerōsthai (not yet to have been made manifest — perfect passive infinitive: the perfect indicates the sustained non-disclosure): the Spirit intentionally encoded the limitation of access into the tabernacle's structure as a theological statement: the way into God's presence was blocked, not because the first tabernacle was temporary but because the sacrifice that opens the way had not yet been made.
9: hētis parabolē eis ton kairon ton enestēkota — "this is an illustration for the present time" (parabolē: parable, type, illustration — from para + ballō: to throw alongside; a placing-beside-for-comparison): the tabernacle is explicitly called a parabolē, and the "present time" (kairon ton enestēkota) is the time of the new covenant's inauguration. The tabernacle's function as parable/type was always its primary theological function. Kata syneidēsin (in relation to conscience — syneidēsis: the shared-knowledge/conscience, the inner moral awareness): the Levitical gifts and sacrifices could not achieve the cleansing that matters — the inner moral-spiritual conscience — only external ceremonial status.
10: monon epi brōmasin kai posasin kai diaphorois baptismois, dikaiōmata sarkos mechri kairou diorthōseōs epikeimenoi — "they are only a matter of food and drink and various ceremonial washings — external regulations applying until the time of the new order" (dikaiōmata sarkos: ordinances/regulations of flesh — sarx here = the bodily/material dimension; fleshly ordinances as opposed to the spiritual transformation that conscience requires): mechri kairou diorthōseōs (until the time of the setting-right/correction — diorthōsis: a setting-upright, a reformation, a restoration to the correct condition; only here in the NT): the Levitical system was designed with a temporal limit — mechri (until): its validity was always provisional, awaiting the kairos (the appointed time) of the new order.
13: ei gar to haima tragōn kai taurōn kai spodos damaleos rhantizouśa tous kekoinōmenous hagiaizei pros tēn tēs sarkos katharotēta — "if the blood of goats and bulls and the ashes of a heifer sprinkled on those who are ceremonially unclean sanctify them for the purification of the flesh" (spodos damaleos: ashes of a heifer — the red heifer ritual of Num 19; the ashes mixed with water were sprinkled on those defiled by corpse-contact): kekoinōmenous (those having been defiled — perfect passive: their defilement is an established condition; koinoō: to make common, to defile by contamination): pros tēn tēs sarkos katharotēta (for the cleanness of the flesh): the Levitical purification achieves external, ritual cleanliness — the body is made ceremonially fit for worship — but does not address the moral-spiritual interior.
14: posō mallon to haima tou Christou, hos dia pneumatos aiōniou heauton prosēnegken amōmon tō theō, katharizei tēn syneidēsin hymōn apo nekrōn ergōn eis to latreuein theō zōnti — "how much more, then, will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself unblemished to God, cleanse our consciences from acts that lead to death, so that we may serve the living God" (posō mallon: how much more — the a minori ad maius argument [if the lesser, how much more the greater]): dia pneumatos aiōniou (through the eternal Spirit — aiōniou: belonging to the coming age, eternal): the Spirit's involvement in the self-offering is not merely instrumental but constitutive of its quality — the sacrifice transcends the temporal order by virtue of the Spirit's eternal character. Heauton prosēnegken amōmon (he offered himself unblemished — the reflexive: the priest and the offering are one; amōmos: without spot or blemish, the sacrificial requirement): katharizei tēn syneidēsin (cleanses the conscience — syneidēsin): where the Levitical blood achieved external cleansing, Christ's blood reaches the syneidēsis — the inner moral awareness.
16: hopou gar diathēkē, thanaton anankē pheresthai tou diathemenou — "for where there is a will/covenant, the death of the one who made it must be established" (diathēkē: will/testament; also covenant — the Greek word covers both): here Hebrews exploits the Greek legal sense of diathēkē as a last will and testament, which only takes effect at death. Thanaton anankē pheresthai (it is necessary for a death to be brought forward/established — pheresthai: to be borne, produced as evidence; the passive suggests a legal presentation of proof): the death of the testator is the condition for the will's validity. This legal logic is applied to the covenant: the new covenant's benefits require the death of the covenant-maker.
17: diathēkē gar epi nekrois bebaia, epei mēpote ischyei hote zē ho diathemenos — "because a will is in force only when somebody has died; it never takes effect while the one who made it is living" (epi nekrois bebaia: confirmed/secure over/upon the dead — bebaia: legally firm, secure; same bebaios root used throughout for reliability): the legal observation is the argumentative bridge: the old covenant was dedicated with blood at Sinai (v18-20), and the new covenant is inaugurated through the death of its mediator. Both covenants require death for their validity.
18: hothen oude hē prōtē chōris haimatos enkekainistai — "this is why even the first covenant was not put into effect without blood" (enkekainistai: has been inaugurated, dedicated — perfect passive of enkainizō: to inaugurate, to make new, to consecrate by a fresh act; the kaino-root: the same word group as kainē diathēkē [new covenant]): the irony is noted: the first covenant was enkainizō-d (inaugurated/made new) with blood, and the new covenant is likewise inaugurated with the blood of its mediator. The blood-inauguration pattern is continuous across both covenants.
19: lalētheisēs gar pasēs entolēs kata ton nomon hypo Mōyseōs panti tō laō, labōn to haima tōn moschōn kai tōn tragōn meta hydatos kai eriou kokkinou kai hyssōpou auto te to biblion kai panta ton laon errantisen — "when Moses had proclaimed every commandment of the law to all the people, he took the blood of calves, together with water, scarlet wool and branches of hyssop, and sprinkled both the scroll and all the people" (Exodus 24:6-8 with some elaboration): Hebrews includes details not in the Exodus narrative (calves and goats, water, scarlet wool, hyssop) — possibly drawing on Num 19 or later Jewish tradition. Auto te to biblion (both the scroll itself): the sprinkling of the scroll is not in Exod 24 — Hebrews may be indicating that the written law itself was consecrated with blood, showing the intimate connection between covenant-document and covenant-blood.
20: legōn, Touto to haima tēs diathēkēs hēs eneteilato pros hymas ho theos — "saying: This is the blood of the covenant, which God has commanded you to keep" (Exodus 24:8 LXX): Touto to haima tēs diathēkēs (This is the blood of the covenant): the formula that Jesus reuses at the Last Supper (Matthew 26:28; Mark 14:24; Luke 22:20; 1 Corinthians 11:25) — the verbal link is deliberate and Hebrews' readers would recognize it. The Sinai blood-of-the-covenant becomes the interpretive lens for the crucifixion event; the new covenant's blood-dedication recapitulates and surpasses the Sinai pattern.
21: kai tēn skēnēn de kai panta ta skeuē tēs leitourgias tō haimati homoiōs errantisen — "in the same way, he sprinkled with the blood both the tabernacle and everything used in its ceremonies" (homoiōs: in the same way, likewise): the tabernacle's blood-dedication extended to all its vessels and furnishings. Hebrews' summary of Levitical inauguration practice goes beyond Exod 24 — the Mosaic consecration of the tabernacle (Exod 40) and its furnishings apparently involved blood in tradition, though the Exodus narrative focuses on anointing oil. The pattern is: everything in the old covenant system had its existence mediated through blood.
23: anankē oun ta men hypodeigmata tōn en tois ouranois toutois katharizesthai, auta de ta epourania kreittosi thysiais para tautas — "it was necessary, then, for the copies of the heavenly things to be purified with these sacrifices, but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices than these" (kreittosi thysiais: better sacrifices — plural): the plural is unexpected since Christ offered one sacrifice. Hebrews uses the plural rhetorically to match the comparison: since the earthly needed purification with multiple sacrifices, the heavenly required "better" (single but superior) ones. Alternatively, the plural may encompass all aspects of Christ's sacrificial work. Anankē (necessity): the purification of the heavenly things is necessary because the heavenly sanctuary is the one where Christ presents his self-offering — it must be purified to function as the ultimate Holy of Holies.
25: oud' hina pollakis prospherē heauton, hōsper ho archiereus eiserchetai eis ta hagia kat' eniauton en haimati allotriō — "nor did he enter heaven to offer himself again and again, the way the high priest enters the Most Holy Place every year with blood that is not his own" (kat' eniauton: year by year, annually — the annual cycle of Day of Atonement entry): en haimati allotriō (with blood belonging to another — allotrios: belonging to another person, foreign, not one's own): the contrast is precise: the Levitical high priest must bring someone else's blood because his own would be unfit; Christ enters with his own blood because he is both priest and sacrifice. The repetition of entry reveals the incompleteness; the once-for-all entry declares the completion.
26: epei edei auton pollakis pathein apo katabolēs kosmou; nyn de hapax epi synteleia tōn aiōnōn eis athetēsin tēs hamartias dia tēs thysias autou pephanerōtai — "otherwise Christ would have had to suffer many times since the creation of the world. But he has appeared once for all at the culmination of the ages to do away with sin by the sacrifice of himself" (epi synteleia tōn aiōnōn: at the completion/end of the ages — synteleia: joint-completion, the convergence of all the ages at their end-point): the incarnation and sacrifice are placed at the hinge-point of history — the ages converge on this event. Eis athetēsin tēs hamartias (for the setting-aside of sin — athetēsis: the same legal annulment term used in 7:18 for the setting-aside of the former commandment): sin is legally annulled, not merely covered over.
27: kai kath' hoson apokeitai tois anthrōpois hapax apothansein, meta de touto krisis — "just as people are destined to die once, and after that to face judgment" (apokeitai: it is laid away/stored up/appointed — from apokeimai: what is reserved and waiting; death is humanity's standing appointment): hapax apothansein (to die once): the human "once" of death mirrors the divine "once" of Christ's sacrifice in v28 — the typological pairing is the rhetorical point: just as humanity dies once and faces judgment once, so Christ was offered once and will appear once for salvation. The universal human pattern (one death, one judgment) is the frame for the particular divine act (one sacrifice, one appearing).
